content.text: System prompts are production code: they determine Claude's behavior, must be versioned, and must pass quality checks before reaching production users. A

lets teams publish versioned prompts via a CLI or IDP portal, enforces an eval gate before a prompt can reach production, and makes the full prompt catalog searchable across the organization — enabling reuse and preventing redundant authoring.

attrs.alt: Prompt registry lifecycle: author → registry API → eval gate → versioned store → consumer services + audit log

content.text: Every prompt in the registry is stored as a YAML document with a standard schema. The schema captures the prompt text, the variables it accepts, the Claude model it was authored for, and the eval case file that must pass before a new version is published.

# prompts/invoice_extractor/v1.2.0.yaml
name: invoice_extractor
version: 1.2.0
owner: finance-platform
description: "Extract structured invoice fields (total, due date, vendor) from raw invoice text"
model: claude-sonnet-5
max_tokens: 512

variables:
  - name: invoice_text
    type: string
    description: "Raw invoice text to extract from"
  - name: currency
    type: string
    default: "USD"
    description: "Expected currency code for validation"

system_prompt: |
  You are a precise invoice data extractor. Extract the following fields from the
  invoice text and return valid JSON with no commentary:
  - total: numeric amount ({{ currency }} dollars)
  - due_date: ISO 8601 date string
  - vendor_name: string
  - invoice_number: string

  If a field is not present in the text, set it to null.
  Return only the JSON object — no explanation text.

eval_cases: s3://acme-prompts/evals/invoice_extractor/v1.2.0/cases.jsonl
eval_pass_threshold: 0.90
tags:
  - extraction
  - finance
  - structured-output

# Validate the prompt schema locally (no API call)
acme prompt validate prompts/invoice_extractor/v1.2.0.yaml

# Upload eval cases to S3 (done once per version)
aws s3 cp evals/cases/invoice_extractor_v1.2.0.jsonl \
  s3://acme-prompts/evals/invoice_extractor/v1.2.0/cases.jsonl

# Publish: runs eval gate, then registers in the registry
acme prompt publish prompts/invoice_extractor/v1.2.0.yaml

# Output:
# ✓ Schema validation passed
# ✓ Eval gate: running 50 cases against claude-sonnet-5...
# ✓ Eval gate: avg_score=0.94 ≥ 0.90 threshold — passed
# ✓ Prompt invoice_extractor:1.2.0 published
# ✓ Audit record written (publisher: alice@acme.com)
#
# Consumers can now fetch this prompt:
#   acme prompt get invoice_extractor --version 1.2.0
#   acme prompt get invoice_extractor --channel stable   (resolves latest stable)

# prompt_registry/api.py
import yaml, json, boto3, time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from acme_auth.fastapi import get_current_user_claims

app = FastAPI(title="Prompt Registry")
_ddb = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table("prompt-registry")
_s3 = boto3.client("s3")
BUCKET = "acme-prompts"


class PublishRequest(BaseModel):
    prompt_yaml: str   # full YAML content as a string


@app.post("/prompt/publish")
async def publish_prompt(req: PublishRequest, claims: dict = Depends(get_current_user_claims)):
    # 1. Parse and validate schema
    try:
        spec = yaml.safe_load(req.prompt_yaml)
    except yaml.YAMLError as e:
        raise HTTPException(400, f"Invalid YAML: {e}")
    required = ["name", "version", "system_prompt", "eval_cases", "eval_pass_threshold"]
    missing = [f for f in required if f not in spec]
    if missing:
        raise HTTPException(400, f"Missing required fields: {missing}")

    # 2. Check for duplicate version
    existing = _ddb.get_item(Key={"pk": spec["name"], "sk": spec["version"]})
    if "Item" in existing:
        raise HTTPException(409, f"{spec['name']}:{spec['version']} already exists")

    # 3. Run eval gate (synchronous — caller waits)
    eval_result = _run_eval_gate(spec)
    if not eval_result["passed"]:
        raise HTTPException(422,
            f"Eval gate failed: avg_score={eval_result['avg_score']:.2f} < "
            f"{spec['eval_pass_threshold']} threshold")

    # 4. Store in S3 and register in DynamoDB
    key = f"prompts/{spec['name']}/{spec['version']}.yaml"
    _s3.put_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=key, Body=req.prompt_yaml.encode())
    _ddb.put_item(Item={
        "pk": spec["name"],
        "sk": spec["version"],
        "s3_key": key,
        "published_by": claims["email"],
        "published_at": int(time.time()),
        "eval_score": str(eval_result["avg_score"]),
        "tags": spec.get("tags", []),
        "description": spec.get("description", ""),
    })
    return {"status": "published", "name": spec["name"], "version": spec["version"]}
Consumer services fetch prompts from the registry at service startup (for low-latency) or per-call (for A/B testing). The registry client caches prompt content in memory with a TTL.

# acme_claude/prompts.py — included in the internal SDK wrapper
import time, boto3, yaml, functools
from typing import Literal

_ddb = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table("prompt-registry")
_s3 = boto3.client("s3")
BUCKET = "acme-prompts"

Channel = Literal["stable", "beta", "latest"]

class PromptCache:
    def __init__(self, ttl_seconds: float = 300.0):
        self._ttl = ttl_seconds
        self._cache: dict[str, tuple[dict, float]] = {}   # (spec, fetched_at)

    def get(self, name: str, version: str | None = None, channel: Channel = "stable") -> dict:
        cache_key = f"{name}:{version or channel}"
        entry = self._cache.get(cache_key)
        if entry and time.monotonic() - entry[1] < self._ttl:
            return entry[0]
        spec = self._fetch(name, version, channel)
        self._cache[cache_key] = (spec, time.monotonic())
        return spec

    def _fetch(self, name: str, version: str | None, channel: Channel) -> dict:
        if version is None:
            # Resolve channel to latest version with that tag
            resp = _ddb.query(
                KeyConditionExpression="pk = :name",
                ExpressionAttributeValues={":name": name},
                ScanIndexForward=False,   # latest first
                Limit=1,
            )
            if not resp["Items"]:
                raise KeyError(f"No prompt found: {name}")
            version = resp["Items"][0]["sk"]
        key = f"prompts/{name}/{version}.yaml"
        content = _s3.get_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=key)["Body"].read().decode()
        return yaml.safe_load(content)

_default_cache = PromptCache()

def get_prompt(name: str, version: str | None = None) -> dict:
    """Fetch a prompt spec from the registry. Cached in-process for 5 minutes."""
    return _default_cache.get(name, version)


# Usage:
# spec = get_prompt("invoice_extractor", version="1.2.0")
# system_prompt = spec["system_prompt"].replace("{{ currency }}", "USD")
Jinja2-style placeholders. The registry client provides a render function that validates that all required variables are supplied before substitution:

# acme_claude/prompts.py (continued)
def render_prompt(spec: dict, variables: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """
    Render the system_prompt template with the given variable values.
    Raises ValueError if any declared variable without a default is missing.
    """
    declared = {v["name"]: v for v in spec.get("variables", [])}
    # Check all required variables are present
    missing = [
        name for name, var in declared.items()
        if name not in variables and "default" not in var
    ]
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Missing required prompt variables: {missing}")
    # Apply defaults for optional variables
    merged = {v["name"]: v.get("default", "") for v in spec.get("variables", [])}
    merged.update(variables)
    # Simple substitution (no nested expressions; use Jinja2 for complex logic)
    result = spec["system_prompt"]
    for key, value in merged.items():
        result = result.replace(f"{{{{ {key} }}}}", str(value))
    return result
The registry supports three named channels alongside explicit version pinning:
Latest version with no beta tag

Production services must pin to an explicit version (e.g. 1.2.0) or the stable channel. The latest channel bypasses eval-gate history — if a bad prompt is published and immediately promoted to latest, it reaches production without a per-deployment eval check.

# Service startup: pin to stable channel, log the resolved version
from acme_claude.prompts import get_prompt, render_prompt

PROMPT_SPEC = get_prompt("invoice_extractor")   # resolves stable channel
RESOLVED_VERSION = PROMPT_SPEC["version"]
print(f"Loaded invoice_extractor:{RESOLVED_VERSION}")

def extract_invoice(invoice_text: str, currency: str = "USD") -> dict:
    system = render_prompt(PROMPT_SPEC, {"invoice_text": invoice_text, "currency": currency})
    ...

The Internal Developer Portal provides a searchable catalog of all published prompts. Teams can browse by tag or owner, view the rendered prompt text, see the eval score history, and fork a prompt as the starting point for their own version.
: full-text search across name, description, and tags
: copy an existing prompt into your service namespace with one click — preserves the original's eval case set as a starting baseline
: side-by-side diff between two versions of the same prompt
: chart of eval scores across versions — a drop in score on a new version is visible before it reaches production
: which services are currently consuming each prompt version

# Mark a version deprecated (consumers still resolve it, but get a warning)
acme prompt deprecate invoice_extractor --version 1.1.0 \
  --message "Use 1.2.0 — improved extraction of multi-currency invoices"

# Consumers fetching the deprecated version receive:
# WARNING: invoice_extractor:1.1.0 is deprecated.
# Use 1.2.0 — improved extraction of multi-currency invoices

# List all prompts with deprecated versions in use
acme prompt audit --show-deprecated
The prompt registry stores versioned system prompts as YAML documents with a standard schema covering name, version, variables, model target, and eval case reference.
Every publish runs an eval gate: if the average case score falls below the declared threshold, the publish is rejected — prompts cannot regress to production without passing quality checks.
Consumer services fetch prompts at startup or per-call via a cached registry client; the render function validates that all declared variables are supplied before substitution.
Three channels (stable, beta, latest) let services track prompt releases without updating their code; production services should pin to stable or an explicit version.
The IDP prompt catalog makes all published prompts searchable and forkable across the organization, reducing duplicated prompt authoring and surfacing reusable patterns.
